title: Information Security
description: Downloaded Information Security JSON exams imported from Downloads.

exams.title: Lecture 2 - Introduction to Information Security
exams.description: Lecture Exams - Fundamental concepts, history, CIA, CNSS/McCumber model, and the security development life cycle.

questions.prompt: Which statement best matches the lecture definition of information security?
choices: Protecting information and its critical elements, including the systems that use, store, and transmit it
Encrypting every file regardless of business need
Replacing all human procedures with software controls
questions.explanation: Information security protects confidentiality, integrity, availability, and the supporting systems, using policy, technology, training, and awareness.

questions.prompt: Which items are named as critical elements in the classic CIA triangle?
questions.explanation: The CIA triangle is confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

questions.prompt: What did early mainframe and military computing security focus on first?
choices: Physical controls against theft, espionage, and sabotage
questions.explanation: The history section explains that early computer security began with physical controls at sensitive locations.

questions.prompt: The lecture presents ARPANET as a system that had no early security concerns because user identification was mature.
questions.explanation: The lecture says ARPANET had fundamental security problems, including weak dial-up safety and missing user identification and authorization.

questions.prompt: The NSTISSC/CNSS Security Model is also known as which model?
questions.explanation: The slides identify the NSTISSC/CNSS Security Model as the McCumber Cube.

questions.prompt: A company trains staff, writes password policy, and deploys access-control software. Which lecture idea does this best demonstrate?
choices: Defense using policy, technology, and training/awareness together
questions.explanation: The information security definition emphasizes policy, technology, training, and awareness working together.

exams.title: Lecture 2 Hard Quiz - Introduction to Information Security - 30 Questions
exams.description: Lecture Exams - Hard closed-question quiz on security scope, CIA, history, CNSS/McCumber Cube, and SecSDLC.

questions.prompt: A manager says buying antivirus completes the information security program. Which answer is strongest?
choices: Antivirus is only one technical control; information security also involves information, systems, policies, people, training, and hardware
Antivirus is enough if it is paid
Information security is only network security
Policies matter only after a breach
questions.explanation: The lecture defines information security broadly, not as one tool.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about information security scope?
choices: Information security protects information and critical elements
Supporting systems and hardware are part of the scope
Training and policy are part of protection
People are outside the security scope
questions.explanation: The lecture defines information security broadly, not as one tool.

questions.prompt: Information security protects only computers and ignores people, policy, and procedures.
questions.explanation: The lecture defines information security broadly, not as one tool.

questions.prompt: An attacker copies private student records but does not change or delete them. What is the best CIA classification?
choices: Confidentiality is primarily broken because unauthorized disclosure occurred
Integrity is primary because files were copied
Availability is primary because the attacker used network bandwidth
Authentication is the CIA goal being broken
questions.explanation: Confidentiality concerns unauthorized disclosure.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about confidentiality?
choices: Confidentiality is mainly about accurate data
questions.explanation: Confidentiality concerns unauthorized disclosure.

questions.prompt: Which answer best corrects the mistake 'copying data is not a security breach'?
choices: Copying private data can break confidentiality even when integrity and availability remain intact
Copying data is only physical loss
questions.explanation: Confidentiality concerns unauthorized disclosure.

questions.prompt: A grade record is changed from 88 to 58, but the system remains online and no one else sees the record. Which CIA goal is strongest?
choices: Integrity, because the information was changed without authorization
Confidentiality, because the grade is private
Authentication, because all grade problems are login problems
questions.explanation: Integrity protects accuracy and authorized modification.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about integrity?
choices: Backups can help restore integrity after corruption
Integrity means the website is online
questions.explanation: Integrity protects accuracy and authorized modification.

questions.prompt: Integrity is mainly about preventing unauthorized reading of information.
questions.explanation: Integrity protects accuracy and authorized modification.

questions.prompt: A registration portal crashes during enrollment, but no records are stolen or modified. Which answer is best?
choices: Availability is primarily affected because users cannot access the service when needed
Confidentiality is primary because students are involved
Integrity is primary because registration is important
questions.explanation: Availability is about timely access to systems and data.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about availability?
choices: Backups and redundancy can support availability
Power, network, and server failures can harm availability
questions.explanation: Availability is about timely access to systems and data.

questions.prompt: Which control is most directly tied to availability?
questions.explanation: Availability is about timely access to systems and data.

questions.prompt: A question asks why early computer security began with guards, locked rooms, and restricted locations. Which answer fits the lecture?
choices: Early mainframes and military systems first needed physical controls against theft, espionage, and sabotage
Early security began with smartphone app permissions
ARPANET had perfect authorization from the beginning
The Internet made physical security unnecessary
questions.explanation: The lecture traces security from physical controls to broader computer and information security.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about history of information security?
choices: ARPANET exposed identification and authorization weaknesses
R-609 expanded security beyond physical protection
Security began only after cloud computing
Early networks had no misuse potential
questions.explanation: The lecture traces security from physical controls to broader computer and information security.

questions.prompt: Information security began only after modern social media attacks.
questions.explanation: The lecture traces security from physical controls to broader computer and information security.

questions.prompt: An exam asks why Rand Report R-609 matters. Which answer avoids the common trap?
choices: It helped broaden computer security to data safety, unauthorized access limits, and organizational involvement
It proved physical security is unnecessary
questions.explanation: R-609 is historically important because it expanded the scope of computer security.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about Rand Report R-609?
choices: It is linked to the formal study of computer security
It moved the scope beyond only physical security
It included concern for unauthorized data access
It focused only on firewall packet filtering
It ended the need for management involvement
questions.explanation: R-609 is historically important because it expanded the scope of computer security.

questions.prompt: Which answer is the best exam summary of R-609?
choices: A milestone in expanding security thinking beyond physical protection
questions.explanation: R-609 is historically important because it expanded the scope of computer security.

questions.prompt: A student says the McCumber Cube is just another name for the CIA triangle. What is the best correction?
choices: CIA is one dimension; the cube also includes information states and safeguards
The cube is only a firewall model
The cube is only an SDLC model
questions.explanation: The McCumber Cube is three-dimensional.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about McCumber Cube dimensions?
choices: Storage, processing, and transmission are information states
Policy, training, and technology are safeguards
Programming languages are a cube dimension
The cube gives exact firewall commands
questions.explanation: The McCumber Cube is three-dimensional.

questions.prompt: The McCumber Cube has only one dimension: confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
questions.explanation: The McCumber Cube is three-dimensional.

questions.prompt: A file is encrypted on disk, checked while being edited, and sent through HTTPS. Which McCumber idea is being applied?
choices: The same information is considered across storage, processing, and transmission states
This is unrelated to the McCumber Cube
questions.explanation: Information states are storage, processing, and transmission.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about information states?
choices: Transmission means data moving across a network
questions.explanation: Information states are storage, processing, and transmission.

questions.prompt: Which option is an information state?
questions.explanation: Information states are storage, processing, and transmission.

questions.prompt: A university protects grades with a policy, teacher training, and access-control software. Which answer is best?
choices: It combines policy, education/training/awareness, and technology safeguards
Training is not part of security
questions.explanation: The cube includes multiple safeguard types.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about safeguards and countermeasures?
questions.explanation: The cube includes multiple safeguard types.

questions.prompt: The McCumber Cube treats technology as the only valid safeguard.
questions.explanation: The cube includes multiple safeguard types.

questions.prompt: A team finishes a portal, then asks whether login, logging, backups, and authorization are needed. Which critique is best?
choices: Security was treated as an afterthought instead of being integrated through the life cycle
Security should always wait until deployment
Backups are useful only before design
questions.explanation: SecSDLC integrates security into every phase.

questions.prompt: Match the item to the best concept: 1) unauthorized reading, 2) unauthorized changing, 3) system offline, 4) security across storage/processing/transmission.
choices: 1 confidentiality; 2 integrity; 3 availability; 4 McCumber Cube information states
questions.explanation: Unauthorized disclosure is confidentiality, unauthorized modification is integrity, downtime is availability, and the cube tracks information states.

questions.prompt: Fill in the blank: The CNSS/NSTISSC Security Model is also known as the ____.
questions.explanation: The CNSS/NSTISSC model is commonly called the McCumber Cube.

exams.title: Lecture 3 - Objectives and Threats
exams.description: Lecture Exams - Business needs for information security and major threat categories against information assets.

questions.prompt: What is the primary mission of information security according to the lecture?
choices: Ensure systems and their contents stay reliable and protected
questions.explanation: The lecture frames information security around keeping systems and contents dependable in the face of daily attacks.

questions.prompt: Which are listed business functions of information security?
choices: Protect the organization ability to function
questions.explanation: The slides list protecting functionality, enabling safe applications, protecting data, and safeguarding technology assets. Risk cannot be eliminated permanently.

questions.prompt: Which phrase best defines a threat?
choices: A confirmed loss that already happened
An object, person, or entity representing constant danger to an asset
questions.explanation: A threat is a potential danger to an asset; it may or may not become an attack.

questions.prompt: The lecture describes intellectual property as mainly involving what?
choices: Ownership of ideas and control over their tangible or virtual representations
questions.explanation: The IP section focuses on ideas and their representations, with software piracy as a common breach.

questions.prompt: Which examples are deliberate software attacks from the lecture?
questions.explanation: Viruses, worms, and Trojan horses are malware examples. An approved patch is maintenance, not an attack.

questions.prompt: An ISP outage prevents customers from reaching a hosted service. Which threat category fits best?
questions.explanation: Internet, communication, and power irregularities affect availability and are treated as quality-of-service deviations.

exams.title: Lecture 3 Hard Quiz - Objectives and Threats - 30 Questions
exams.description: Lecture Exams - Hard closed-question quiz on business needs, threat categories, IP compromise, malware, QoS deviations, and human/technical threats.

questions.prompt: A hospital says security is optional because doctors can still work around broken systems manually. Which answer is best?
choices: Security protects the organization’s ability to function, so business operations are directly involved
Security is only an IT preference
Security matters only after data is public
questions.explanation: The lecture says information security is a management and people issue.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about business function protection?
choices: Security should be evaluated by business impact and cost
Security is never a management issue
questions.explanation: The lecture says information security is a management and people issue.

questions.prompt: Information security is only an IT department issue and does not concern business function.
questions.explanation: The lecture says information security is a management and people issue.

questions.prompt: A payroll app runs but allows any employee to view all salaries. Which business function is most directly missing?
choices: Safe operation of applications on IT systems
Only interface usability, with no effect on security controls
questions.explanation: Safe operation means correct protection, not just uptime.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about safe application operation?
choices: Application security is unrelated to information security
Once software runs, it is automatically safe
questions.explanation: Safe operation means correct protection, not just uptime.

questions.prompt: Which is the best sign an application is not operating safely?
choices: Users can access data outside their need or role
questions.explanation: Safe operation means correct protection, not just uptime.

questions.prompt: A company loses its transaction records but keeps all laptops. Which answer is strongest?
choices: The organization may lose its record of business and ability to deliver value even if hardware remains
Data loss is not a security issue
questions.explanation: Data is central to business records and services.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about data protection?
choices: Data at rest must be protected
Data in motion must be protected
Data is often central to organizational value
Stored data is never a target
Transmitted data does not need protection
questions.explanation: Data is central to business records and services.

questions.prompt: An organization without its data is unaffected if it still owns its computers.
questions.explanation: Data is central to business records and services.

questions.prompt: A weak password exists on a server, but no one has used it yet. How should it be classified?
choices: The weak password is a vulnerability; attackers or events that could exploit it are threats
The weak password itself is a completed attack
No security issue exists until damage occurs
It is only a QoS deviation
questions.explanation: The lecture separates threats from attacks and weaknesses.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about threat definition?
choices: An attack is an action that exploits weakness
Threat, vulnerability, and attack are identical
questions.explanation: The lecture separates threats from attacks and weaknesses.

questions.prompt: Which statement correctly separates threat and attack?
choices: Threat is potential danger; attack is an action against an asset
questions.explanation: The lecture separates threats from attacks and weaknesses.

questions.prompt: A student installs cracked commercial software from an unofficial site. What is the best classification before considering malware?
choices: Compromise of intellectual property through software piracy
questions.explanation: Software piracy is a common IP compromise.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about intellectual property compromise?
choices: Software piracy is a common IP breach
SIIA and BSA are watchdog organizations mentioned in this context
IP means only hardware serial numbers
questions.explanation: Software piracy is a common IP compromise.

questions.prompt: Software piracy is mainly a firewall architecture problem.
questions.explanation: Software piracy is a common IP compromise.

questions.prompt: A program pretending to be a game opens a hidden remote access channel after installation. Which threat category fits best?
choices: Deliberate software attack, specifically a Trojan horse/back door style risk
questions.explanation: Malware is designed to damage, disrupt, steal, or deny service.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about deliberate software attacks?
choices: Trojan horses are deliberate software attacks
Malware is always accidental user error
questions.explanation: Malware is designed to damage, disrupt, steal, or deny service.

questions.prompt: Which set contains only deliberate software attack examples?
questions.explanation: Malware is designed to damage, disrupt, steal, or deny service.

questions.prompt: A web application is secure but unreachable because the outsourced hosting provider has an outage. Which classification is strongest?
choices: Confidentiality breach because hosting is external
questions.explanation: QoS deviations come from support-service failures.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about quality-of-service deviations?
choices: QoS failures always mean data was stolen
questions.explanation: QoS deviations come from support-service failures.

questions.prompt: Quality-of-service problems cannot affect information security.
questions.explanation: QoS deviations come from support-service failures.

questions.prompt: An employee accidentally emails a private spreadsheet to the wrong mailing list. Which answer is best?
choices: Human error causing a confidentiality incident
No risk because it was accidental
questions.explanation: Threats include people and accidental failures, not just malicious attackers.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about human error?
choices: Accidents are excluded from information security
questions.explanation: Threats include people and accidental failures, not just malicious attackers.

questions.prompt: Which is the best human-error example?
choices: A user misconfigures permissions and exposes private files
A guard checks a badge correctly
A server room door locks automatically
questions.explanation: Threats include people and accidental failures, not just malicious attackers.

questions.prompt: An insider opens confidential strategy documents without permission but does not alter them. Which answer is strongest?
choices: Espionage or trespass primarily affecting confidentiality
Integrity because opening always changes files
questions.explanation: Espionage/trespass involves unauthorized access.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about espionage or trespass?
questions.explanation: Espionage/trespass involves unauthorized access.

questions.prompt: Espionage or trespass can only happen from outside the organization.
questions.explanation: Espionage/trespass involves unauthorized access.

questions.prompt: A worm consumes bandwidth, an ISP fails, and a power outage occurs. What is the best shared impact?
choices: All can reduce availability even though they come from different threat categories
questions.explanation: CIA impacts can overlap across threat categories.

questions.prompt: Match the scenario: 1) ISP outage, 2) cracked software, 3) employee sends file to wrong person, 4) virus infection.
choices: 1 QoS deviation; 2 intellectual property compromise; 3 human error; 4 deliberate software attack
1 integrity only; 2 availability only; 3 no risk; 4 policy
questions.explanation: The lecture distinguishes QoS, IP compromise, human error, and malware/software attacks.

questions.prompt: Fill in the blank: Software piracy is a common compromise of ____ property.
questions.explanation: Software piracy is an intellectual property issue.

exams.title: Lecture 4 - Hardware and Physical Security
exams.description: Lecture Exams - Information system components, hardware as an asset, physical loss, and layered facility controls.

questions.prompt: Which components are part of an information system in the lecture?
questions.explanation: The lecture lists six IS components: software, hardware, data, people, procedures, and networks.

questions.prompt: Why is unrestricted physical access so dangerous?
choices: It usually means the system cannot guarantee information security
It removes the need for encryption
It affects only replacement cost, not confidentiality or availability
questions.explanation: Physical access can bypass many logical controls and threatens hardware, data, and system trust.

questions.prompt: Which are listed sources of physical loss?
questions.explanation: The slides list extreme temperature, gases, liquids, living organisms, projectiles, movement, and energy anomalies.

questions.prompt: What is tailgating in physical security?
choices: Following an authorized person through an access point without authorization
Running a firewall in transparent mode
Sending many packets to exhaust bandwidth
Copying a password hash for offline cracking
questions.explanation: Tailgating occurs when an unauthorized individual follows an authorized user through a controlled entry.

questions.prompt: A small enclosure with separate entry and exit points used to verify a person before facility entry is a:
questions.explanation: A mantrap controls entry by holding a person in an enclosure until verification succeeds or security overrides.

questions.prompt: Electronic monitoring is useful, but the lecture notes it is often reactive and may not prevent access by itself.
questions.explanation: Monitoring records events and supports investigation, but recordings may not be watched in real time and do not automatically prevent activity.

exams.title: Lecture 4 Hard Quiz - Hardware and Physical Security - 30 Questions
exams.description: Lecture Exams - Hard closed-question quiz on information system components, hardware risks, physical loss, tailgating, mantraps, monitoring, alarms, and facility controls.

questions.prompt: A student says an information system is only hardware plus software. Which correction is strongest?
choices: It also includes data, people, procedures, and networks
It is only the server room
questions.explanation: The lecture lists six IS components.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about information system components?
choices: Only application users, not administrators or procedures
Only physical devices, not data or networks
questions.explanation: The lecture lists six IS components.

questions.prompt: Data is not an information system component.
questions.explanation: The lecture lists six IS components.

questions.prompt: A stolen laptop costs little but contains private records. Which answer is best?
choices: The data risk can be greater than the device cost
questions.explanation: Hardware loss often creates data exposure risk.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about hardware value versus data value?
questions.explanation: Hardware loss often creates data exposure risk.

questions.prompt: Which control most directly reduces confidentiality harm if a laptop is stolen?
choices: A visible asset tag without encryption or screen lock
questions.explanation: Hardware loss often creates data exposure risk.

questions.prompt: A server has strong passwords, but anyone can boot it from USB in an unlocked room. What is the strongest judgment?
choices: Logical controls are weakened by unrestricted physical access
This is only a QoS deviation
The room being unlocked improves availability
questions.explanation: Physical access can undermine technical controls.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about unrestricted physical access?
choices: Physical access is unrelated to information security
questions.explanation: Physical access can undermine technical controls.

questions.prompt: Strong passwords always eliminate physical-access risk.
questions.explanation: Physical access can undermine technical controls.

questions.prompt: Cooling fails, rodents chew cables, and a water pipe leaks. What do these share?
choices: They are physical loss sources that can damage hardware or availability
They are all intellectual property breaches
questions.explanation: Physical loss sources include environmental and physical hazards.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about physical loss sources?
choices: Temperature is a physical loss source
Living organisms are a physical loss source
Liquids are a physical loss source
Strong passwords are physical loss sources
User roles are physical loss sources
questions.explanation: Physical loss sources include environmental and physical hazards.

questions.prompt: Which is not a physical loss source from the lecture?
questions.explanation: Physical loss sources include environmental and physical hazards.

questions.prompt: A person with no authorization enters by walking behind someone carrying boxes. Which control issue is strongest?
choices: Tailgating bypassed the controlled access point
questions.explanation: Tailgating is unauthorized physical following.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about tailgating?
choices: It involves following an authorized user
Badges alone may not stop it
questions.explanation: Tailgating is unauthorized physical following.

questions.prompt: Tailgating is a type of malicious software.
questions.explanation: Tailgating is unauthorized physical following.

questions.prompt: An attacker has a copied badge, but a guard notices the face does not match. What is shown?
choices: Human judgment can strengthen badge-based physical security
questions.explanation: Badges need supporting controls.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about badges and guards?
choices: Layered controls are stronger than one control
Guards remove all need for procedures
questions.explanation: Badges need supporting controls.

questions.prompt: Which answer best explains why guards are useful?
choices: They can assess unusual situations and respond with judgment
They are purely automated controls with no human judgment
questions.explanation: Badges need supporting controls.

questions.prompt: A visitor enters a small enclosure, is checked, and only then can exit into the data center. What is the best term?
questions.explanation: A mantrap is a physical access-control enclosure.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about mantraps?
choices: A mantrap has controlled entry and exit
It verifies access before allowing entry
A mantrap is a password type
questions.explanation: A mantrap is a physical access-control enclosure.

questions.prompt: A mantrap is a network firewall mode.
questions.explanation: A mantrap is a physical access-control enclosure.

questions.prompt: CCTV captures a break-in, but the guard sees it later. What is the exam trap?
choices: Monitoring helped evidence collection but was reactive in this case
questions.explanation: Monitoring may detect or record rather than prevent.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about electronic monitoring?
questions.explanation: Monitoring may detect or record rather than prevent.

questions.prompt: Which control is most reactive if reviewed only later?
questions.explanation: Monitoring may detect or record rather than prevent.

questions.prompt: An alarm sounds when a wiring closet door is forced open. Why is the closet sensitive?
choices: Network devices and cables inside can be disrupted or abused
Wiring closets contain cabling but no security-relevant assets
questions.explanation: Computer rooms and wiring closets contain critical infrastructure.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about alarms and sensitive rooms?
choices: Wiring closets are public by design
Alarms remove the need for response
questions.explanation: Computer rooms and wiring closets contain critical infrastructure.

questions.prompt: Unauthorized access to a wiring closet cannot affect information security.
questions.explanation: Computer rooms and wiring closets contain critical infrastructure.

questions.prompt: A lock exists but is broken, so staff prop the door open. Which answer is best?
choices: The control exists in name but fails operationally, so alternative procedures are needed
questions.explanation: Locks need maintenance and supporting procedures.

questions.prompt: Match the scenario: 1) person follows employee, 2) two-door entry, 3) power spike, 4) footage reviewed after theft.
choices: 1 tailgating; 2 mantrap; 3 energy anomaly; 4 reactive monitoring
questions.explanation: These are the main physical-security trap terms.

questions.prompt: Fill in the blank: A ____ is the best answer when a question asks for a controlled two-door physical verification area.
questions.explanation: A mantrap is a controlled two-door physical entry space.

exams.title: Lecture 5 - Software and Personal Computer Security
exams.description: Lecture Exams - Software attacks and failures, OWASP risks, secure SDLC, software assurance, and portable devices.

questions.prompt: Why does the lecture call software one of the hardest IS components to secure?
choices: It has no connection to attacks
Programming errors account for a substantial portion of attacks
It is never used by people
questions.explanation: Software flaws, bugs, and design mistakes create many exploitable weaknesses.

questions.prompt: Trap doors in software are best described as:
choices: Purposeful shortcuts that bypass security checks
Physical doors to a server room
questions.explanation: The lecture defines trap doors as intentional shortcuts left by programmers that can bypass security checks.

questions.prompt: Which items are OWASP-style web application risks mentioned in the lecture?
questions.explanation: The lecture lists risks such as injection, broken authentication, sensitive data exposure, security misconfiguration, and XSS.

questions.prompt: Software assurance mainly means:
choices: Building security into the development life cycle
Buying antivirus instead of writing secure code
questions.explanation: Software assurance aims to build security into the SDLC so software can be deployed with confidence.

questions.prompt: Which SDLC phases should consider security in the NIST approach from the lecture?
questions.explanation: Security must be considered across the whole life cycle, including disposal.

questions.prompt: Which design principle is the best correction for giving every user administrator rights by default?
choices: Least privilege: programs and users should use only necessary privileges
Open access: every user should own every object
Obscurity only: hide the login page
Manual patching only: avoid design controls
questions.explanation: Least privilege reduces damage by granting only the privileges needed for a task.

questions.explanation: Risk identification through asset identification, classification, categorization, and prioritization This is the strongest interpretation for asset identification.

questions.prompt: Which answer best avoids a common exam mistake about asset identification?
choices: Risk identification through asset identification, classification, categorization, and prioritization
questions.explanation: Risk identification through asset identification, classification, categorization, and prioritization This is the strongest interpretation for asset identification.

questions.prompt: Two systems face similar threats, but one supports emergency hospital care. What should change?
choices: Priority should reflect business importance and impact, not just technical similarity
Both must always receive identical priority
The less important system is always first
questions.explanation: Priority should reflect business importance and impact, not just technical similarity This is the strongest interpretation for asset classification and prioritization.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about asset classification and prioritization?
choices: All public and private assets are equal
questions.explanation: Priority should reflect business importance and impact, not just technical similarity This is the strongest interpretation for asset classification and prioritization.

questions.prompt: Risk priority should ignore asset value and mission impact.
questions.explanation: Priority should reflect business importance and impact, not just technical similarity This is the strongest interpretation for asset classification and prioritization.

questions.prompt: A team asks which threats are realistic, whether they are internal or external, and how likely they are. Which step is this?
questions.explanation: Threat assessment within risk assessment This is the strongest interpretation for threat assessment.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about threat assessment?
choices: Every imaginary threat gets equal weight
questions.explanation: Threat assessment within risk assessment This is the strongest interpretation for threat assessment.

questions.prompt: Which answer best avoids a common exam mistake about threat assessment?
questions.explanation: Threat assessment within risk assessment This is the strongest interpretation for threat assessment.

questions.prompt: A scan finds exposed services and weak configurations that could let threats succeed. Which assessment is this?
choices: Vulnerability assessment because weaknesses in assets and controls are identified
questions.explanation: Vulnerability assessment because weaknesses in assets and controls are identified This is the strongest interpretation for vulnerability assessment.

questions.prompt: Which statements are correct about vulnerability assessment?
questions.explanation: Vulnerability assessment because weaknesses in assets and controls are identified This is the strongest interpretation for vulnerability assessment.

questions.prompt: A vulnerability assessment identifies the attackers' motivations but never system weaknesses.
questions.explanation: Vulnerability assessment because weaknesses in assets and controls are identified This is the strongest interpretation for vulnerability assessment.

questions.prompt: A company patches one risk, buys insurance for another, accepts a low risk, and stops a risky service. What is shown?
choices: Different treatment strategies: mitigate, transfer/share, accept, and avoid/terminate
questions.explanation: Different treatment strategies: mitigate, transfer/share, accept, and avoid/terminate This is the strongest interpretation for risk treatment strategies.

questions.prompt: Match the concept: 1) identify assets, 2) analyze severity, 3) decide acceptability, 4) choose response.
choices: 1 risk identification; 2 risk analysis; 3 risk evaluation; 4 risk treatment
1 threat assessment; 2 vulnerability assessment; 3 asset classification; 4 risk acceptance
1 establish context; 2 identify assets; 3 analyze likelihood/impact; 4 transfer/share risk
questions.explanation: The first option is the only mapping that matches the lecture concepts precisely.

questions.prompt: Fill in the blank: Risk ____ decides what action brings risk to an acceptable level.
questions.explanation: Risk treatment selects actions to bring risk within acceptable limits.

exams.title: Reference - Principles of Information Security, 4th Ed.
exams.description: Reference Exams - Reference concepts that support the early lectures, business needs, CNSS model, SSLC, threats, and risk.

questions.prompt: Why do the lectures repeatedly cite Principles of Information Security?
choices: It supplies the course frameworks, terminology, and examples
It is only a programming syntax manual
It is unrelated to risk management
questions.explanation: The lecture slides cite the textbook as a source for core information security principles and frameworks.

questions.prompt: A textbook-style security program usually starts with business needs because:
choices: Controls should protect mission, data, applications, and technology assets
Technical controls should ignore organizational goals
questions.explanation: Security decisions should be tied to business impact, cost, and organizational function.

questions.prompt: Which textbook concepts align with the CNSS/McCumber view?
choices: Information states such as storage, processing, and transmission
Security goals such as confidentiality, integrity, and availability
Countermeasures such as policy, training, and technology
questions.explanation: The CNSS/McCumber model combines security goals, information states, and safeguards.

questions.prompt: A policy is written, staff are trained, and a technical control is configured for the same risk. What principle is shown?
choices: Layered safeguards across management, people, and technology
questions.explanation: Textbook security programs combine administrative, technical, and physical safeguards.

questions.prompt: In the risk chapters, an asset should be prioritized mainly by:
choices: Its importance and value to the organization
Whether it is easy to ignore
questions.explanation: Asset priority depends on business importance, sensitivity, replacement cost, and impact.

questions.prompt: Which are useful outputs of a textbook-style risk assessment?
choices: A promise that no future attack can happen
questions.explanation: Assessment produces knowledge for decisions; it cannot guarantee zero future attacks.

questions.prompt: A control that reduces the likelihood or impact of a threat is part of:
questions.explanation: Controls are chosen to reduce risk to an acceptable level.

questions.prompt: The reference material treats information security as both a management issue and a technology issue.
questions.explanation: The lectures and textbook framing both emphasize management responsibility, people, policy, and technology.

exams.title: Reference - Principles of Information Security, 7th Ed.
exams.description: Reference Exams - Reference concepts that support hardware/software security, malware, IDPS, cryptography, access control, and firewalls.

questions.prompt: The 7th edition reference is used most heavily in these lectures for:
choices: Updated protection technologies and security software topics
questions.explanation: Many later lectures cite the 7th edition for hardware, software, malware, IDPS, cryptography, access control, and firewall material.

questions.prompt: Which controls belong to a layered facility defense?
questions.explanation: The physical security material uses multiple layers such as locks, badges, monitoring, alarms, and controlled rooms.

questions.prompt: Why does modern malware defense combine signatures and behavior?
choices: Known malware and unknown suspicious activity require different detection approaches
Signatures detect every future attack perfectly
questions.explanation: Signature-based tools handle known threats; behavior helps detect new or modified threats.

questions.prompt: Which access control model reduces admin work when users change jobs?
questions.explanation: RBAC ties privileges to roles, making job changes easier to administer.

questions.prompt: A proxy firewall is valuable because it:
choices: Can break the direct connection and inspect application-level traffic
questions.explanation: Application gateways/proxies mediate traffic at the application layer.

questions.prompt: Which algorithms or terms belong to cryptography from the reference-aligned lecture?
questions.explanation: AES, public-key systems, and hash/HMAC concepts are cryptographic; tailgating is physical security.

questions.prompt: A host records suspicious file changes and alerts the admin. Which reference topic is most relevant?
questions.explanation: Host-based IDPS monitors activity on a particular computer or server.

questions.prompt: The reference material supports the idea that no single technical control is enough for complete security.
questions.explanation: Security needs layered controls, governance, maintenance, user behavior, and ongoing assessment.

exams.title: Reference - Information Security in Simple Language
exams.description: Reference Exams - Awareness-focused review inspired by the Arabic reference: practical security habits, human risk, and basic protections.

questions.prompt: A simple-language information security reference is most useful for:
choices: Explaining practical security ideas to non-specialists
questions.explanation: Awareness references help users understand everyday risks and safer behavior.

questions.prompt: Which habits are basic personal information security practices?
questions.explanation: Strong passwords, MFA, and updates reduce common risks. Verification codes should not be shared.

questions.prompt: A message pressures a user to click a link and enter account details. Which concept fits best?
questions.explanation: Phishing uses deception and urgency to steal credentials or trigger unsafe actions.

questions.prompt: Why are backups important in practical security?
choices: They support recovery after deletion, malware, or system failure
They are the same as passwords
questions.explanation: Backups preserve recoverability when availability or integrity is harmed.

questions.prompt: Security awareness matters because people can accidentally bypass strong technical controls.
questions.explanation: Users can be tricked, choose weak passwords, lose devices, or approve unsafe prompts.

questions.prompt: A safe response to an unexpected attachment from an unknown sender is to:
choices: Avoid opening it and verify the sender through a trusted channel
questions.explanation: Unknown attachments can carry malware; verification and caution are safer.

questions.prompt: Which actions help protect mobile or portable devices?
choices: Leaving the device unattended in public
questions.explanation: Portable devices are easily stolen, so lock, encryption, and recovery controls help.

questions.prompt: The simplest explanation of confidentiality is:
choices: Only authorized people can access the information
questions.explanation: Confidentiality protects information from unauthorized disclosure.

exams.title: All Lectures Exam 1 - Foundations and Threats
exams.description: Comprehensive Final Exams - Mixed review from introduction, business needs, CIA, threats, malware, and physical security.

questions.prompt: Which option is a complete CIA set?
questions.explanation: CIA is confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

questions.prompt: A laptop is stolen from a coffee shop, exposing customer records. Which two themes are most directly involved?
questions.explanation: The device is physical hardware and the exposed records are a confidentiality issue.

questions.prompt: Which term means a weakness such as a missing patch or weak configuration?
questions.explanation: A vulnerability is a weakness that can be exploited.

questions.prompt: A worm differs from a virus because a worm:
choices: Can replicate without a host program
Requires a host document macro only
questions.explanation: Worms self-propagate; viruses attach to hosts.

questions.prompt: Which are examples of layered physical controls?
questions.explanation: Layered physical security uses several controls so one failure does not expose the facility.

questions.prompt: An outsourced hosting provider has a major outage. Which security objective is affected first?
questions.explanation: Outages prevent access to systems and information, so availability is harmed.

questions.prompt: The Rand Report R-609 is important because it:
choices: Helped expand computer security beyond physical security toward data and access concerns
questions.explanation: The lecture presents R-609 as a starting point for broader computer security study.

questions.prompt: If no threats existed, security resources could focus only on improving systems rather than defending them.
questions.explanation: Lecture 3 makes this point to explain why threat awareness shapes security priorities.

exams.title: All Lectures Exam 2 - Software, Malware, and Security Software
exams.description: Comprehensive Final Exams - Mixed review from software failures, OWASP, malware, antivirus, and IDPS.

questions.prompt: Which OWASP risk involves untrusted input being interpreted as commands or queries?
questions.explanation: Injection occurs when malicious input is treated as executable command/query structure.

questions.prompt: Which controls or practices belong to secure software development?
choices: Consider security in each SDLC phase
Add security only after users complain
questions.explanation: Secure SDLC and design principles build security into software instead of treating it as an afterthought.

questions.prompt: A back door is:
choices: A hidden access point into a system
questions.explanation: Back doors/trap doors bypass normal security paths and are hard to detect.

questions.prompt: A program looks useful but installs a remote access payload when opened. What is it?
questions.explanation: Trojans hide malicious behavior inside apparently useful software.

questions.prompt: Which antivirus technique depends most on frequent database updates?
questions.explanation: Signature tools compare files to known malware patterns, so updates matter.

questions.prompt: IDPS is best described as:
choices: Systems that detect and may prevent intrusions
questions.explanation: IDPS combines intrusion detection and prevention capabilities.

questions.prompt: Which are consequences of DoS/DDoS attacks?
questions.explanation: DoS and DDoS attack availability by overwhelming systems or networks.

questions.prompt: A team says antivirus makes patching unnecessary. What is the best correction?
choices: Antivirus is one layer; patching and secure configuration are still required
Patching is only for physical locks
Updates should be disabled to avoid change
questions.explanation: No single control is enough. Malware defense, patching, configuration, monitoring, and awareness work together.

exams.title: All Lectures Exam 3 - Protection Techniques
exams.description: Comprehensive Final Exams - Mixed review from cryptography, steganography, access control, authentication, and authorization.

questions.prompt: Which control protects confidentiality by making intercepted content unreadable?
questions.explanation: Encryption transforms plaintext into ciphertext unreadable to unauthorized users.

questions.prompt: Which key model is used by symmetric encryption?
questions.explanation: Symmetric encryption uses the same secret key for encryption and decryption.

questions.prompt: Which are examples of authentication factors?
questions.explanation: Authentication factors map to something you know, have, and are.

questions.prompt: Which access control model is mandatory and classification-driven?
questions.explanation: Mandatory Access Control uses required classifications/sensitivity levels assigned by the system.

questions.prompt: A user is authenticated, then the system checks what files the user may read. Which function is this second check?
questions.explanation: Authorization maps an authenticated identity to allowed assets and actions.

questions.prompt: Which technique hides a message inside another medium so the message existence is less obvious?
questions.explanation: Steganography conceals the existence of the message.

questions.prompt: Hash functions are mainly used to support:
questions.explanation: Hashes produce digests useful for integrity verification and related security functions.

questions.prompt: A manager says identification, authentication, and authorization are identical. What is the best correction?
choices: Identification claims an identity, authentication proves it, authorization grants permitted actions
They are all only encryption types
They apply only to physical doors
Authorization happens before a user is known
questions.explanation: Access control separates identity claim, proof, and permission decisions.

exams.title: All Lectures Exam 4 - Firewalls and Risk Management
exams.description: Comprehensive Final Exams - Mixed review from firewall technologies, risk framework, asset assessment, and treatment.

questions.prompt: A packet filtering firewall usually works at which focus?
choices: Headers such as source/destination, ports, and protocols
questions.explanation: Packet filtering uses header fields to allow or block traffic.

questions.prompt: Which firewall type is most associated with proxy behavior?
questions.explanation: Application gateways are also called proxy servers.

questions.prompt: Which are valid ways to categorize firewalls?
questions.explanation: The lecture categorizes firewalls by type, model/form factor, and mechanism/technique.

questions.prompt: Risk identification must include asset classification because:
choices: Different assets have different importance and sensitivity
Classification prevents all attacks by itself
questions.explanation: Classification helps prioritize effort based on value and sensitivity.

questions.prompt: The team reviews laws, suppliers, internal processes, and business goals before scoring risk. Which step is this?
questions.explanation: Context includes external and internal factors affecting the risk management process.

questions.prompt: Risk evaluation asks:
choices: Is the current risk level acceptable?
Which cipher has the prettiest name?
questions.explanation: Evaluation judges whether analyzed risk is acceptable or requires treatment.

questions.prompt: Which are common risk treatment choices?
questions.explanation: Risk treatment can reduce, transfer, accept, or avoid risk depending on context and appetite.

questions.prompt: A firewall can reduce network risk, but it does not remove the need for risk management.
questions.explanation: Firewalls are controls inside a broader risk management program.

exams.title: All Lectures Exam 5 - Final-Style Challenge
exams.description: Comprehensive Final Exams - Course-wide closed-choice challenge across all lectures and references.

questions.prompt: A visitor follows an employee through a badge-controlled door. Which control failure happened?
questions.explanation: Tailgating bypasses a physical access point by following an authorized user.

questions.prompt: Which attacker variant uses hacking as digital protest?
questions.explanation: Hacktivists use hacking skills for political or social protest.

questions.prompt: Which pairings are correct?
choices: Availability: keep systems/data accessible when needed
questions.explanation: The three CIA goals map to disclosure, modification, and access/uptime concerns.

questions.prompt: A cloud database of malware indicators helps which antivirus technique?
questions.explanation: Cloud protection uses cloud intelligence and faster updates to detect threats.

questions.prompt: Which is the best example of accountability?
choices: Logs tie actions to a specific authenticated user
Everyone uses the same shared account
questions.explanation: Accountability requires actions to be attributable and reviewable.

questions.prompt: A user says, “I can share my password because we also have a firewall.” What is the best correction?
choices: Passwords authenticate users and should never be shared; firewalls do not replace identity controls
Sharing is safe if the firewall is expensive
Passwords are a form of steganography
questions.explanation: Network controls do not make credential sharing safe.

questions.prompt: Which item is a threat source rather than a control?
questions.explanation: A black hat attacker is a human threat source; the others are controls.

questions.prompt: Which questions belong in risk management?
choices: Where and what is the risk?
What treatment brings risk to an acceptable level?
questions.explanation: These match the framework questions in Lecture 12.

questions.prompt: Which concept best explains why security uses multiple layers?
choices: Any single control can fail or have limited scope
One control always blocks all attacks
questions.explanation: Layering reduces dependence on a single control.

questions.prompt: The course connects technical controls with policy, training, management, and user behavior.
questions.explanation: This is a recurring theme across the lectures and references.
